Marketing Analytics Dashboard

This dashboard is created using the weekly sales of 44 stock-keeping units (SKUs) from a tech-gadget e-commerce retailer over a period of 100 weeks, from October 2016 to September 2018.
Tips: Click and drag the portion of some graphs of your choice to zoom in and out. Double-click to reset. Select different SKUs from the dropdown to show different forecast.
Edward Lim, Fabricio Rodriguez Peña, Rafia Al-Jassim, Rathes Waran, Wenyuan Yun

Welcome to the Marketing Analytics Dashboard

This dashboard analyses 44 SKUs from a tech-gadget e-commerce retailer across 100 weeks of sales data. It is organised into three analytical tabs. Here is how to navigate and interpret each one:

1. Demand Forecasting

Select any SKU from the dropdown to see its historical sales and predicted future demand. Four models compete: a naïve moving-average baseline plus three ML models (Linear Regression, Random Forest, Neural Network) — the best-performing model (lowest MAE on the held-out test set) is automatically highlighted.

  • Interpretation box — Plain-English summary of expected demand, trend direction, and the 95% confidence range.
  • Forecast chart — The shaded band around the best model’s forecast is the 95% CI: it widens with horizon, reflecting increasing uncertainty.
  • Forecast weeks slider — Adjust to look 2–12 weeks ahead.
  • Test size slider — Controls how many recent weeks are held out for evaluation. Smaller values give more training data; larger values give a longer comparison window. Consistent performance across test sizes builds confidence in the forecast.
  • Residuals & Feature Importance — Diagnostics panel showing prediction errors and which features (price, promotion, trend, quarter, price change) drive each SKU’s demand most.
  • All-SKU Heatmap — Portfolio-level view of Random Forest demand forecasts for all 44 SKUs across the next 4 weeks.

2. Promotion Effectiveness

This tab answers: “Do feature promotions actually boost sales, and by how much?”

We use the SCAN*PRO model (Van Heerde et al., 2002) — the standard marketing science framework for measuring promotion effectiveness from observational data. A simple comparison of promoted vs non-promoted weeks would be confounded by simultaneous price discounts, seasonal patterns, and demand trends. SCAN*PRO controls for all three, isolating the pure promotion effect.

  • Portfolio KPIs — Show how many SKUs have a statistically significant promotion effect.
  • Per-SKU detail — Select a SKU to see its promotion coefficient (β₂), the estimated sales lift, and incremental units generated per promo week.
  • Lift chart — Ranks all SKUs by incremental sales. Blue bars = significant positive lift; red = significant negative; grey = not significant.
  • ★ symbol — Indicates statistical significance (p < 0.05), meaning we can be confident the effect is real.

3. Price Elasticity

This tab answers: “How sensitive is each SKU’s demand to price changes, and what happens to revenue if we change price?”

Elasticity is estimated using a Scan*Pro log-log OLS model per SKU, controlling for promotions, trend, and seasonality. The coefficient ε directly gives price elasticity.

  • Elasticity (ε) — A value of −2 means a 10% price rise causes a ~20% demand drop. ε below −1 = elastic (price-sensitive); between −1 and 0 = inelastic. A ★ means the result is statistically significant (p < 0.05).
  • Overview sub-tab — Portfolio-level bar chart, distribution histogram, and pie chart showing how many SKUs fall into each elasticity category. Full results table with p-values and R².
  • SKU Deep Dive — Per-SKU demand and revenue curves, a waterfall chart of revenue impact at standard scenarios, and a full what-if scenario table. The ★ on the revenue curve marks the revenue-maximising price.
  • Scenario Testing — Standard ±5%, 10%, 20%, 30% impact table with demand and revenue bar charts. Use the custom slider (−30% to +30%) to simulate any price change and see demand and revenue impact instantly.
  • Portfolio Analysis — Category-level box plots, a ranking chart of all SKUs by elasticity with 95% confidence-based colour coding, a revenue impact heatmap across all SKUs and scenarios, and a pricing strategy recommendation table.
  • Pricing rule of thumb: Elastic SKUs (ε < −1) grow revenue by cutting price; inelastic SKUs (ε between −1 and 0) grow revenue by raising price.

General Tips

  • Click and drag on any Plotly chart to zoom in. Double-click to reset.
  • Hover over data points for detailed tooltips.
  • Use the SKU dropdowns on each tab to switch between products.
  • Green numbers indicate positive outcomes; red indicates negative.
4
8

Forecast Detail

Diagnostics

All-SKU Forecast Heatmap (Random Forest)

Our Methodology — Demand Forecasting

Data: 44 SKUs × 98 weeks from the processed dataset.

Feature engineering — economically motivated, minimal set:

  • price — the primary demand driver.
  • feat_main_page — binary promotion indicator.
  • trend — linear time index capturing long-run growth/decline.
  • quarter — captures seasonal rhythm with 1 variable instead of 11 month dummies. With ~90 rows per SKU, 11 binary dummies mostly read as noise; a single quarter variable is more sample-efficient.
  • price_change — current price minus last week’s price. Captures the reference-price effect (“did price just go up or down?”) without the multicollinearity of including raw lagged prices. Raw price levels (price, price-1, price-2) are ~95% correlated (VIF > 50), but the current level and the change from last week are nearly uncorrelated.

Why no lagged prices? price-1 and price-2 are ~95% correlated with current price (VIF > 50). They exist for the SCAN*PRO model (reference-price formation, stockpiling) but for demand forecasting, current price already carries the price signal. Including them causes multicollinearity without adding predictive information.

Per-SKU training: With only 5 features, each SKU’s ~90 training rows give a 18:1 sample-to-feature ratio — healthy for all three model types without pooling. Category pooling was tested but removed: SKUs within the same category have different demand levels and dynamics, so the pooled model learned the category average rather than each SKU’s individual behaviour.

Naïve baseline (Moving Average): Predicts the average of the last 8 training weeks. This is the standard retail forecasting benchmark. If an ML model cannot beat the naïve baseline, it is overfitting or the data has no learnable structure beyond recent history. Including it makes model comparison honest.

Hyperparameter tuning: Random Forest and MLP hyperparameters selected via GridSearchCV with TimeSeriesSplit (3 folds) on category-pooled data with the same 5-feature set the models will use in production.

Models:

  • Naïve (Moving Avg) — No features. Benchmark baseline.
  • Linear Regression — OLS on raw features. Scale-invariant.
  • Random Forest — Tuned via CV on category-pooled data.
  • Neural Network (MLP) — Tuned via CV, StandardScaler-normalised inputs.

Confidence intervals: σresidual × 1.96 × √(step) — widens with forecast horizon (Hyndman & Athanasopoulos, 2021).

Feature importance (RF-based): The diagnostics section shows Random Forest’s importance ranking. RF importance is scale-invariant, captures non-linear effects, and is robust to multicollinearity — unlike LR coefficients (scale-dependent) or MLP weights (opaque). This tells the marketing manager which levers most influence each SKU’s demand.

All-SKU heatmap (RF-based): The portfolio heatmap uses RF because it offers non-linear flexibility, requires no preprocessing (unlike MLP), and averages across many trees for robustness. For the per-SKU analysis, all four models are shown for honest comparison.

Best model selection: Lowest MAE on the held-out test set — naïve baseline included.

References: Cohen, M.C. et al. (2022). Demand Prediction in Retail. Springer SSCM 14. · Hyndman, R.J. & Athanasopoulos, G. (2021). Forecasting: Principles and Practice, 3rd ed. OTexts.

Portfolio Promotion Overview

Incremental Sales Lift — All SKUs

Promotion Effectiveness Summary

Our Methodology — Feature Promotion Effectiveness

The question: Does featuring a product on the platform’s main page actually boost sales? And if so, by how much?

Why not just compare promoted vs non-promoted weeks? Because the comparison is confounded: retailers often discount and feature products simultaneously (price confounding), promotions cluster around high-demand periods like holidays (seasonal confounding), and if demand is growing over time, later promotional weeks look artificially good (trend confounding). We need a model that controls for all three.

Why SCAN*PRO? The SCAN*PRO model (Van Heerde et al., 2002) was specifically designed for measuring promotion effectiveness from observational retail data. It is the standard in marketing science for this application. We use OLS because the goal is coefficient estimation (measuring β₂, the promotion effect), not prediction. OLS gives unbiased, interpretable estimates with valid standard errors for hypothesis testing.

Model:

ln(salesit) = α + β₁·ln(priceit) + β₂·featit + β₃·trendt + β₄·ln(pricei,t-1) + β₅·ln(pricei,t-2) + Σγk·monthk + εit

Why each feature is included:

  • ln(pricet) — Separates the price discount effect from the promotion effect. Without it, β₂ captures both, overstating the promotion’s own contribution.
  • feat_main_page — The variable of interest. Binary (0/1), so it enters linearly — exp(β₂) − 1 = proportional sales lift.
  • trend — Prevents attributing demand growth to promotions if they cluster in later weeks.
  • ln(pricet-1), ln(pricet-2) — SCAN*PRO’s defining feature. Captures reference-price formation and stockpiling. Van Heerde et al. (2002) showed that omitting price lags biases both β₁ and β₂.
  • month dummies — Removes seasonal demand variation so β₂ reflects genuine promotion response, not holiday peaks.

Incremental sales: Lift = eβ₂ − 1; incremental/week = Q₀ × lift; total = incremental/week × promoted weeks.

Significance: OLS t-test on β₂ (p < 0.05). CI: exp(β₂ ± 1.96·SE) − 1.

References: Van Heerde et al. (2002). Schmalenbach Business Review, 54, 198–220. · Hanssens et al. (2001). Market Response Models, 2nd ed. Kluwer. · Van Heerde et al. (2004). Marketing Science, 23(3), 317–334.

Price Strategy Summary

Our Methodology — Price Elasticity & Scenario Testing

The question: How sensitive is each SKU’s demand to price changes?

Model: Scan*Pro log-log OLS with HC1 robust standard errors, fitted per SKU.

log(sales) = β₀ + β₁·log(price) + β₂·feat + β₃·trend + Σγ·month + ε

β₁ = price elasticity directly. No lagged prices — we estimate steady-state elasticity for what-if scenarios.

Scenario: Qnew = Q₀×(Pnew/P₀)ε, Rnew = Pnew×Qnew. Elastic (|ε|>1): cut price → grow revenue. Inelastic (|ε|<1): raise price → grow revenue.

Classification: Inelastic (|ε|<1), Moderately Elastic (1≤|ε|<2), Highly Elastic (|ε|≥2).

References: Van Heerde et al. (2002). Schmalenbach Business Review, 54. · Hanssens et al. (2001). Market Response Models, 2nd ed.

Marketing Analytics Dashboard · Demand Forecasting (scikit-learn) + Price Elasticity (Scan*Pro OLS)
